summary: Stancil argues that analytics leaders get stuck on a career treadmill just below the C-suite because the chief data/analytics role is defined as an operational, second-class position. He proposes splitting the function like engineering does: keep a VP of data for management and create a chief analytics officer who sits with executives to do strategic, high-leverage analysis. This would improve executive decision-making while giving strong analysts a real top-level career path.
money_quotes: The terminal point of an analyst’s career, it seems, is the data leader treadmill, hopping between different VP roles, always running but never moving forward.
There is no role committed to this work, and no title that acknowledges its value.
Data departments should follow the same pattern.
Adding a quiet observer to the executive team is a small price to pay for consistently putting the full force of the company’s best analysts behind its most pressing problems.
What I’ve been looking for, it turns out, may have been here the whole time. I just needed to rewrite it to make it my own.
key_insight: The analytics function needs a true executive-level analyst role—peer to the CTO—to unlock strategic impact and provide a viable top-tier career path for analysts.
